IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST REPUBLIC
OF SRI LANKA
In the matter of an Appeal against a Judgment of
the Provincial High Court under and in terms of
the provisions of the Constitution of Sri Lanka,
High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions)
Act No. 19 of 1990, as amended by High Court of
the Provinces (Special Provisions) Amendment
Act No. 54 of 2006 and other related Laws.

R. Krishnamoorthy,

47/2, Dunbar Road, Hatton.

Vs
Secretary,
Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council,
Hatton.
Petitioner-Respondent-Respondent
Before : D. THOTAWATTA, J.
K. M. S. DISSANAYAKE, J.
Counsel : K.V.S. Ganesharajan with M. Mangaleshwary Shanker and Vithusha Logananthan for the Respondent-Petitioner-Appellant.
Janajith De Silva for the Petitioner-Respondent-Respondent.
Argued on : 08.09.2025
Written Submissions
of the Respondent-
Page 3 of 14
Petitioner-Appellant
tendered on : 01.09.2025
Written Submissions
of the Petitioner-Respondents
-Respondents
tendered on : 24.11.2025
Decided on : 20.03.2026
K. M. S. DISSANAYAKE, J.
The instant appeal arises from an order of the learned High Court Judge of the
Central Province holden at Nuwaraeliya dated 14.06.2019 (hereinafter called and
referred to as ‘the Order’) made in a revision application bearing No.
H.C.A.07/2017/Revision wherein, the learned High Court Judge of the Central
Province holden at Nuwaraeliya (hereinafter called and referred to as ‘the learned
High Court Judge of the Province’) had dismissed the application in revision
preferred thereto by the Respondent-Petitioner-Appellant (hereinafter called and
referred to as ‘the Appellant’) against the order of the learned Magistrate of
Hatton dated 10.03.2017, whereby, he had made order authorizing the
Respondent to demolish the 4th and the 5th floors of a building as being an
unauthorized construction erected by the Appellant in contravention of the
building plan, approved by the Hatton Dickoya Urban Council and as morefully,
described in the petition preferred thereto by the Petitioner under and in terms
of section 28A(3) of the Urban Development Authority Act No. 41 of 1978 as
amended (hereinafter called and referred to as ‘the Act’) seeking an order for
demolition of the same. Being aggrieved by the order, the Appellant has now,
preferred to this Court the instant appeal on the questions of law as morefully,
set out in paragraph 20(i) to (xvi) of the petition of appeal among any other
Page 4 of 14
substantial questions of law that may be urged by Counsel at the hearing of this
appeal and they may be reproduced verbatim the same as follows;
“i. Is it necessary for the UDA to intervene when the power to grant
permission to construct buildings with ground and three other floors has
been vested with the relevant local authority until 22.01.2017?;
ii. If so, is it legal for the Respondent file this application against the
Appellant?;
iii. Is it legal for a local authority to present an Application under the UDA
Act, when the respective Chairman and Secretary have been named by
office for this specific purpose?;
iv. Are the provisions of Article 4(d) of the Constitution mandatory on the
Local Government Authorities?;
v. Are the Hon. Magistrates and Hon. High Court Judges too mandated by
the said Constitutional provision?;
vi. Thus, is it incumbent on the said Judges to safeguard, intervene and
ensure the said provision?;
vii. Is the discriminatory actions and behavior of the Respondent an
instance where the said Judges should have intervened?;
viii. Is it just and fair for the High Court Judge to say, "...I see no illegality,
impropriety or procedural irregularity in the order of the learned
magistrate..." in the face of obvious discriminatory actions of the
Respondent?;
ix. Is it just and fair for a local authority to prosecute only selected
individuals when there are so many such culprits?;
x. Is it just and fair for the UDA to authorize only selected individuals when
there are so many similar culprits?
Page 5 of 14
xi. Is it fair for the demolition of such a valuable building when it can be
legalized by the payment of a fine?
xii. Is it only just and fair that, the local authority and/ or the UDA
authorizes any unauthorized part considering the value of the same and
the delay of two years to take action?
xiii. Is demolition of a building a just and fair order in the face of
reasonable doubt with regard to the legality of the initial application,
mitigatory circumstances and the availability of alternative options?
xiv. Is it just and fair to comment "If there were unauthorized construction
as pointed out by the petitioner the court must appreciate the bravery of
the respondent to initiate actions to remove those unauthorized
constructions. This may well have been the first. In fact, the respondent
must be commended for her pioneer work." when there is undeniable
evidence to the contrary not denied by even the Respondent?
xv. Should not all the grounds raised in a Revision Application be duly
considered in full before making Judgment?
xvi. Does the existence of exceptional circumstances call for an in-depth
consideration of all related facts?”.
It is in this context, let me now, briefly, set out the facts and circumstances that
according to the Appellant, led to the institution of the instant appeal before this
Court.
The Secretary of the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council being the Petitioner thereto,
who is the Respondent to the instant appeal, had on 08th September 2016,
preferred an application under and in terms of section 28A(3) of the Act in the
Magistrate's Court Hatton citing therein, the Appellant to the instant appeal as
the Respondent thereto, seeking, inter alia, an order to demolish an
unauthorized construction described in her application. It was stated by the
Petitioner thereto, in her application that the Respondent thereto, had on 21st
Page 6 of 14
May 2012, submitted an application to get a building plan approved by the
Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council (පෙ1) and it had been approved on 10th of October
2012 by it subject to certain amendments (පෙ2). It was in her application, alleged
by the Petitioner thereto, that the Respondent thereto, had however, constructed
two stories, namely; 4th and 5th floors, in contravention of the approved building
plan. It was in her application, further stated by the Petitioner thereto, that the
Respondent thereto, had on several occasions, been warned by notices annexed
to her application marked as ‘පෙ3’ to ‘පෙ11’, to demolish the unauthorized
construction, namely; 4th and 5th floors, but it was not complied with by the
Respondent thereto and as a result, a final notice dated 23.06.2016 a copy of
which was annexed to her application marked as ‘පෙ12’ was issued to the
Respondent thereto, thereby directing to demolish the said unauthorized
construction, namely; 4th and 5th floors, and informing him that in the event of
failing so to do, it would be demolished under section 28A(1) of the Act without
any further notice and upon a failure on the part the Respondent thereto, to
comply with the final notice sent to him by the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council,
the Secretary thereto, being the Petitioner had filed the application in the
Magistrate’s Court of Hatton in case bearing No. 3295/2016 under section
28A(3) of the Act praying inter-alia, for a demolition order of the unauthorized
construction erected by the Respondent thereto, namely; 4th and 5th floors and
the Respondent thereto, who had appeared before the Magistrate’s Court of
Hatton upon notice, raised in his statement of objections filed in the Magistrate’s
Court of Hatton along with the documents annexed thereto, marked as ව1 to
ව104, a number of preliminary objections with regard to the maintainability of
the application preferred thereto by the Petitioner thereto. The learned
Magistrate of Hatton had having rejected all the preliminary objections in toto,
made order authorizing the Respondent to demolish the 4th and the 5th floors of
the building as being an unauthorized construction erected by the Appellant in
contravention of the building plan, approved by the Hatton Dickoya Urban
Council and as morefully, described in the petition preferred thereto by the
Page 7 of 14
Petitioner under and in terms of section 28A(3) of the Act. Being aggrieved by the
order of the learned Magistrate of Hatton, the Appellant to the instant appeal
had preferred an application in revision to the High Court of the Central Province
holden at Nuwaraeliya seeking to revise and set aside the order of the learned
Magistrate of Hatton and the learned High Court Judge of the Province had
dismissed the application in revision on the premise, that there was no illegality,
impropriety or procedural irregularity in it. Being aggrieved thereby, the
Appellant had now, preferred the instant appeal to this Court seeking to set aside
both of the orders made by the learned Magistrate of Hatton as well as the
learned High Court Judge of the province on an array of questions of law
enumerated above. However, it is to be observed that of questions of law, only a
few of them as enumerated below had been argued by the learned Counsel for
the Appellant before us at the hearing of the instant Appeal in support thereof,
and thus, the rest of the questions of law so formulated by the Appellant in his
petition of appeal had been abandoned.
It was the position adverted to in the petition filed before the Magistrate Court of
Hatton, by the Respondent to the instant appeal who was the Petitioner in the
proceedings before the Magistrate Court of Hatton that the building application
submitted to the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council (පෙ1) by the Appellant had been
approved on 10.10.2012 by it subject to the amendments by the letter annexed
to the petition filed in the Magistrate Court of Hatton marked as (පෙ2). Point to
be emphasized here is that the position so adverted to, by the Respondent to the
instant appeal who being the Petitioner in the proceedings before the Magistrate’s
Court of Hatton that the building application submitted to the Hatton-Dickoya
Urban Council (පෙ1) by the Appellant had been approved on 10.10.2012 by it,
had clearly, and unequivocally, been admitted and endorsed by the Appellant
too, particularly, in paragraph 23 of his statement of showing cause. Not only
that but also it was further, conceded, admitted and endorsed by the Appellant
in no uncertain terms in paragraphs 10, 24, 25, 26, 27(i), and 31(i), of his
showing cause filed in the Magistrate Court of Hatton too.
Page 8 of 14
However, the Appellant being Respondent to the proceedings before the
Magistrate’s Court of Hatton, had despite his clear, and unequivocal, admission
so made by him in paragraph 23 of his statement of showing cause in particular,
filed in the Magistrate Court of Hatton and also in paragraphs 10, 24, 25, 26,
27(i), and 31(i), thereof, that the building application submitted to the Hatton-
Dickoya Urban Council (පෙ1) by the Appellant had been approved on
10.10.2012 by it, nevertheless, made an entirely, futile attempt to take up a
entirely, different position to that of the one taken up by him as aforesaid in
that it was in his own statement of showing cause, contended by the Appellant
who was the Respondent in the proceedings before the Magistrate’s Court that
the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council had approved another plan on 01.04.2013,
which was the latest plan by which 4th and 5th floors of the building in question
had been approved by the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council which was vehemently,
denied and disputed by the Respondent to the instant appeal who was the
Petitioner to the proceedings in the Magistrate’s Court of Hatton. A copy of the
purported plan dated 01.04.2013 had been annexed to his statement of showing
cause Respondent therein, being Appellant to the instant appeal marked as ව1.
Hence, it clearly, appears to me that the Appellant had endeavoured to take up
two different and contradictory positions in his own statement of showing cause
with regard to the date of the approved building plan so that he had been very
much inconsistent in this regard which alone, entitled the learned Magistrate of
Hatton to totally, reject the two different and contradictory positions so adverted
to by the Respondent in the proceedings before the Magistrate’s Court of Hatton
who now being the Appellant in the instant appeal with regard to the approved
building plan.
There is a further point which would in my opinion, certainly, militate against
the position so adverted to by the Respondent in the proceedings before the
Magistrate’s Court of Hatton that the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council had
approved another plan on 01.04.2013, which was the latest plan by which 4th
Page 9 of 14
and 5th floors of the building in question had been approved by the Hatton-
Dickoya Urban Council and it may now, be examined.
The Petitioner had annexed a number of documentary evidence marked as පෙ3
to පෙ12 to the application filed before the Magistrate Court of Hatton under and
in terms of section 28A(3) of the Act in support thereof. However, it is to be
observed that, although, the Respondent who is the Appellant to the instant
appeal had in the statement of showing cause filed in the Magistrate’s Court of
Hatton, specifically, denied the averments of the petition of the Petitioner filed in
the Magistrate’s Court of Hatton in which the documents marked as පෙ3 to පෙ12
had been referred to, he had nevertheless, clearly, and unequivocally, admitted
for the first time in the averments in paragraph 21 of his written submissions
filed before this Court even after the argument of this appeal had been
concluded, that he had received the Letters marked as පෙ3 to පෙ12 directing him
to remove and demolish the 4th and 5th floors being the unauthorized
construction erected by him on the building in total contravention of the building
plan approved by the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council on 10.10.2012 which
unmistakably, shows that he had been made fully, aware by the Petitioner in the
proceedings before the Magistrate’s Court on behalf of the Hatton-Dickoya Urban
Council that the approved building plan is the building plan approved by the
Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council on 10.10.2012 (Vide-පෙ1 and පෙ2) that the
Respondent in the proceedings before the Magistrate’s Court of Hatton who being
Appellant to the instant appeal had erected the 4th and 5th floors on the building
being an unauthorized construction in total contravention of the building plan
approved by the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council on 10.10.2012.
Upon the receipt of those letters (පෙ3 to පෙ12), the Respondent ought to have
promptly, questioned the right of the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council to send him
those letters, had the allegation contained in those letters (පෙ3 to පෙ12) been
untrue or without any legal basis in view of the position so adverted to by him
was true, namely; that it was not පෙ1 but ව1 which was the latest plan by which
Page 10 of 14
the 4th and 5th Floors of the building were approved by the Hatton-Dickoya Urban
Council, for; this is how a man of ordinary prudence ought to have reacted
promptly, upon receipt of letters of that nature had the contents thereof, been
utter falsehoods or devoid of merit.
However, there had been not an iota of material produced before the Magistrate
Court of Hatton by the Respondent in the proceedings before the Magistrate’s
Court of Hatton who being Appellant to the instant appeal, to show that he acted
so after having received those letters (පෙ3 to පෙ12) which by itself, simply, shows
nothing but his mala-fides by reason of the fact that the Respondent in the
proceedings before the Magistrate’s Court of Hatton who being the Appellant in
the instant appeal, had notwithstanding the receipt of those letters (පෙ3 to පෙ12)
informing him of his unauthorized construction in total contravention of the
building plan approved by the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council as evident from
පෙ1 and පෙ2, proceeded to construct 4th and 5th floors at his own free will thereby,
taking the law onto his hands in the absence of any lawful authorization granted
to him for that purpose by the relevant authority, namely; the Hatton-Dickoya
Urban Council.
Hence, I would hold that the position so adverted to by the Respondent in the
proceedings before the Magistrate’s Court of Hatton who being the Appellant in
the instant appeal, ought to have been rejected in-limine on this ground too, as
rightly, done by the learned Magistrate of Hatton.
There is another point which would I think, militate against the position so
adverted to by the Respondent in the proceedings before the Magistrate Court of
Hatton who being the Appellant to the instant appeal and it may now, be
examined.
The Respondent not only in his statement of showing cause filed in the
Magistrate Court of Hatton but also in the petition filed along with the application
in revision in the High Court of the Central Province holden at Nuwaraeliya and
also in the written submissions filed thereat, and also, in the questions of law
Page 11 of 14
formulated in the petition of appeal filed in this Court, had attempted to show
that there were such other unauthorized constructions in the Hatton-Dickoya
Urban Council area in respect of which no action whatsoever, had been taken by
the Petitioner in the proceedings before the Magistrate Court of Hatton.
This is in my opinion, very much suggestive of the fact that the 4th and 5th floors
of the building in question too, were unauthorized constructions among those
such other unauthorized constructions situated in the Hatton-Dickoya Urban
Council area.
Hence, the position so adverted to by the Petitioner in the proceedings before the
Magistrate’s Court of Hatton that the building plan approved by the Hatton-
Dickoya Urban Council was the one shown and referred to in the පෙ1 and පෙ2
and therefore, 4th and 5th floors of the building are unauthorized constructions,
can in my opinion, sustain both in fact and law. On the other hand, the position
so adverted to by the Respondent in the proceedings before the Magistrate’s
Court of Hatton that the building plan approved by the Hatton-Dickoya Urban
Council was the one shown and referred to in the ව1 and not one shown and
referred to in පෙ1 and පෙ2 is untrue and therefore, cannot sustain both in fact
and law.
Hence, I would see no error in the findings of the learned Magistrate of Hatton
arrived at by him on the point discussed above, and as such the contention so
advanced by the learned Counsel for the Appellant at the hearing of this appeal
by way of submissions oral and written particularly, in paragraphs 22 to 27 of
the written submissions of the Appellant furnished to this Court, is not entitled
to succeed both in fact and law and as such it should be rejected in-limine.
In view of my findings with regard to the contention raised by the learned
Counsel for the Appellant in the submissions oral and written particularly, in
paragraphs 22 to 27 of the written submissions of the Appellant furnished to
this Court, the further contentions raised by the learned Counsel for the
Appellant in paragraphs 11 to 17 of the written submissions thereof at the
Page 12 of 14
hearing of this appeal, which are in my opinion, interwoven and interlinked with
the contention so raised, in paragraphs 22 to 27 of the written submissions of
the Appellant, too, are not entitled to succeed both in fact and law and as such
they too, should be rejected in-limine.
With regard to the contention advanced by the learned Counsel for the Appellant
at the hearing of this Appeal on the violation of the legitimate expectation of the
Appellant, however, upon a careful, consideration of the submissions oral and
written made by the learned Counsel in support thereof, particularly, in
paragraphs 28 to 32 of the written submissions, it clearly, appears that no
argument had been advanced by the learned Counsel for the Appellant in
support of the contention so raised by him and as such it too, is not entitled to
succeed both in fact and law and as such it too, should be rejected in-limine.
Besides, the pertinent question that would arise before us for our consideration
is; How can the Appellant who had constructed the 4th and 5th floors of the
building in total contravention of the building plan approved by the Hatton-
Dickoya Urban Council (පෙ1 and පෙ2) and despite the repeated requests
made to him by the Hatton-Dickoya Urban Council by the letters marked
පෙ3 to පෙ12, the receipt of which were clearly, admitted in paragraph 21 of
the written submissions of the Appellant filed before this Court, to remove
and demolish the 4th and 5th floors so constructed by him in total
contravention of the building plan approved by the Hatton-Dickoya Urban
Council (පෙ1 and පෙ2) thereby, taking the law onto his hand, entertain the
legitimate expectation? I think this question should be answered in the
negative and against the Appellant for the reasons stated hereinbefore and as
such the next, contention advanced by the learned Counsel for the Appellant
based on the alleged violation of legitimate expectation of the Appellant too, is
not entitled to succeed both in fact and law and as such it too, should be rejected.
Let me now, examine the burden of proof in an application made to a Magistrate
Court under and in terms of the provisions of section 28A(3) of the Act seeking
Page 13 of 14
an order for demolition of an unauthorized construction allegedly, erected by a
party noticed under the Act.
In an application made to a Magistrate Court under and in terms of the
provisions of section 28A(3) of the Act seeking an order for demolition of an
unauthorized construction allegedly, erected by a party noticed under the Act,
the only defence available to him is that the construction in question had been
done on a valid permit granted to him by the relevant authority and the burden
of proof thereof, will solely, rest on the party noticed.
However, it is significant to observe that the Appellant had not established before
the Magistrate’s Court of Hatton that the 4th and 5th floors in question had been
constructed by him upon a valid permit issued to him by the Respondent under
and in terms of the provisions of the Act.
Hence, the Appellant had not in any manner, discharged the burden of proof so
rested in him by producing a valid permit in respect of the construction in
question as rightly, held both by the learned Magistrate of Hatton as well as the
learned High Court Judge of the Province.
In view of all the above facts and circumstances, I see no error in the order of the
learned Magistrate of Hatton as rightly, held by the learned High Court Judge of
the Province and therefore, the learned High Court Judge of the Province was
entirely, right in rejecting the application in revision preferred to it by the
Appellant from the order of the learned Magistrate of Hatton on the premise that
there was no illegality, impropriety or procedural irregularity in it.
In the circumstances, I would see no legal basis whatsoever, to interfere with any
of the orders pronounced either by the learned Magistrate or the learned High
Court Judge of the Province.
Hence, I would hold that appeal is not entitled to succeed both in fact and law.
In the result, I would dismiss the appeal with costs, fixed at Rs. 75,000/-
Page 14 of 14
I would thus, affirm both of the orders of the learned Magistrate and the learned
High Court Judge of the Province.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
D. THOTAWATTA, J.
I agree.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
